Overall, my experience has helped me grow academically and adjust to college life.

I honestly do like attending Indiana State University, it suits me quite nicely. I prefer the campus life I have here over other colleges where I believe I would feel overwhelmed. I came here because not only was it close to where I was already living, but it also has a good reputation in psychology. I took advantage of this and am currently attending as an undergrad majoring in psychology.

So far I like the overall feel of the campus. It is a smaller campus so it feel like a tight community. Your classes are small so you can build great connections with your peers around you. You can tell that there is a lot of sycamore pride in the community.

After spending four years at Indiana State University, I couldn't have asked for a more well-rounded undergraduate experience. I was able to participate in athletics, serving as the university mascot for three years. I completed two years of undergraduate research that led me to my dream job directly out of college. I participated in Greek Life as a member of Gamma Phi Beta, where I made lifelong friends and sisters and a support system that I will carry forever.
One thing that sticks out to me about Indiana State University is the accommodating nature of the university. As someone who suffers from many food allergies, I had many options and was never without access to safe and healthy options. I also suffer from a chronic illness, for which I was never punished and was able to complete my work and demonstrate my competence in the area in a way that worked for me and worked around doctor visits and sick days.

⭐ A supportive vibe:
Faculty and staff tend to be approachable. You’re not just lost in a crowd. If you put in effort, people will meet you halfway.
⭐ Strong programs (especially in education, nursing, business, and criminal justice):
These areas get a lot of praise for hands-on learning and good career support.
⭐ Campus life that’s what you make it:
There are plenty of organizations, events, and community stuff — but ISU isn’t a wild party school. It’s more “chill hangout with your people” than “never sleep again.”
⭐ Terre Haute life:
Listen… it’s not a flashy city. But cost of living is low, things are easy to navigate, and students usually say it grows on them. Think cozy, not glamorous.
⭐ Good value:
ISU tends to be affordable and offers strong financial aid and scholarship options, especially for Indiana residents
I have family member who have graduated from Indiana State University. I love the campus and how friendly everyone is. I'm looking forward to attending ISU fall of 2026.

Pilot in flight program. Airport close by and planes readily available with great instructors. Flight plans on weekends and evenings allow time for study and social life. sorority life is very special and classes allow me to go home often even though the ride is 2 hours

I've had a good experience at Indiana State University, and I think others can feel at home here. The classes are good and all the professors I've had really care about your success. Especially if you're an art student, there's a strong sense of community among the students and professors. The people on campus in general are very friendly, and I've always felt safe on campus. My only complaint is that I looked into Indiana State University as a LGBTQ+ friendly college, and that was very much true my first year. Since then, they've done away with rules and events that made me feel so secure last year. Everyone is still accepting, and I've even been able to use my preferred name in the systems for the college, but events such as the Terre Haute Pride festival are no longer on campus. The school has also removed the Latinx and Gender Inclusive Living-Learning communities. Despite these issues, I've had an overall positive experience with the college and recommend it to all!
I'm online/distance learning student. It is great even been online to communicate with the instructors and have them respond promptly, which is very helpful.

My overall experience with Indiana State University has been both supportive and transformative. From the start, the faculty and staff created an environment where I felt encouraged to challenge myself academically while also growing as an individual. The programs are well-structured, offering a balance of rigorous coursework and practical experiences that help connect theory to real-world applications.
I always felt that my education was a shared priority. Indiana State also emphasizes diversity, inclusion, and innovation, which has broadened my perspective.
Overall, my time at Indiana State University has given me both the knowledge and the confidence to pursue my professional goals while fostering personal growth.
There’s nothing that Indiana State needs to change.
Love the campus, nice and clean dorms, one on one help with professors if needed, diversity on campus
The professors were not very cooperative. I have had a different advisor every year, and out of the 4 or 5 I have had, only one has actually helped me with my issues.

My experience at Indiana State University has been wonderful so far. The entire Nursing Education staff has been so welcoming and helpful. I have enjoyed attending this college more than others I have attended previously.
Making college more affordable for middle class students who are paying for college without any financial support from parents.

ai_summary: At Indiana University Indianapolis, students appreciate the vibrant urban campus that offers a multitude of activities, clubs, and career opportunities due to its downtown location. Many reviews highlight the supportive professors and the wealth of academic resources available, such as tutoring centers and specialized programs, which foster a strong learning environment . However, the commuter nature of the school means that student life can feel less engaging, particularly on weekends, as many students go home after classes. Safety concerns, especially related to the surrounding city environment, are frequently mentioned, alongside frustrations about parking costs and limited dining options. The diversity among the student body and a strong emphasis on inclusivity are seen as positives, making it an attractive choice for students seeking both a quality education and a sense of community amidst a bustling city backdrop.

reviews.text: I enjoyed my time at IU Indianapolis. I learned valuable skills that prepared me for work force. I was very active on campus and created a lot of student organizations that helped me meet people and network with the community. The faculty and staff were amazing.

I believe that Indiana University Indianapolis is a really great school to attend. This college is not too big and not too small, so you get the feel of a big campus in some aspects and the feel of a small campus in some aspects. I am specifically here for the healthcare field and IU Indianapolis is a fantastic school to go into healthcare and I would highly recommend it for that.

It’s a pretty nice school. The area is in Indy, so you can’t expect more than what the city provides there. They have a very vast amount of programs. I really love the art school there, Herron, specifically. I think in that area is much better than Bloomington or any other schools nearby really. You have to accustom to the student life there a bit but people are very kind, and I’ve had no issues at all. I’d maybe like the area to looks a bit prettier maybe? For as big of a school as IUI is, you would think they’d have more artsy architecture and artworks around.

My experience during my first year at IUI has been excellent. The school is an easy commute, has a welcoming layout and the educators seem to want every student to succeed. There are numerous opportunities for athletics on multiple levels and also special interest activities.
My experience with housing and dining would be the two areas I think need improvement. But there are also many alternatives to both.

As a first-generation college freshman, I expected my experience at IUI to be much more intimidating than it actually was. This campus has been so easy to get around and integrate into. Out of everything, I enjoy the people and the campus itself the most. Almost everyone I've met at IUI has been extremely kind and helpful; I've had almost zero bad experiences with anyone here. The campus itself is gorgeous throughout the seasons and is very accessible to all sorts of students. The tunnels provide a safe, warm way to navigate through campus in the dark winters, and the greenery can be beautiful to be around in the summer. If I had to change anything, I would change the issues with heating in the buildings. Occasionally, there are issues where the buildings are either too hot or too cold to sit in, but they usually get managed very quickly. Overall, this was the best college choice for me, and I am beyond grateful that I chose to come here.

I really enjoy being a student at Indiana University Indianapolis. I have made great friendships and connections that will allow me a head start into the industry. I love being involved in student organizations on campus that feel like home.

The majority of available social events take place during bad times for me as a working student. The only friends I made were friends of other transfer students. More social events, especially club meetings, should take place on weekends so that working students like myself can attend.

I transferred into Indiana University Indianapolis this year from Northern Michigan University. While the scenery at Northern Michigan University was beautiful (the northern lights), being in a city has its perks. I have my own apartment and am centrally located to do activities in the city. I enjoy going to the university's gym for working out. The classes are interesting. Attending this school is far more challenging based on the prestige and curriculum. Additionally, I have played various sports and having our football team undefeated this year is just the cherry on top.

So far, I'm liking my experience with IUI. I like the diversity. Being a transfer student and coming from a predominantly white college, it's nice seeing the diversity on campus. Even though we're just starting the semester, I do like my teachers here at the Herron School of Art & Design. They are willing to teach you, but also be there for you if you need help. Everyone here has been helpful and accommodating to my new experience being here at IUI. I would like to see a change in the number of majors they have. There used to be more majors now there aren't that many due to the split from Purdue University. Other than that, it's a great school to start your college experience with.

My experience at Indiana University Indianapolis has been incredibly positive and rewarding. From the start, I felt welcomed by the campus environment and the sense of community it offers. I quickly met a great group of friends who share similar goals and values, and we have remained close, supporting each other both academically and personally. I have also built strong connections with my professors, who are approachable, encouraging, and genuinely invested in student success. I enjoy my classes and appreciate how engaging and applicable the material is. IU Indianapolis feels like a place where I belong and continue to grow.

Overall, I love my college. And I am grateful to be here to have a chance to pursue my studying and making my dream, my goals become true. This is a safety place for me to go to study, the professors and faculty staffs are all respect me, my ideas and my questions. They are also willing to help whenever I have questions. The only one thing I am worry is the financial burden, I am immigrant and I live in US by myself, I am really need help with the tuition and expenses. I am trying to apply as much as scholarships as I can. Because I still keep go to school and earn the degree.

There is quite a few things about this university that I enjoy, but my favorite is the way this campus is set up. It has quick and easy access to pretty much all the academic buildings because it is connected throughout the campus by overhead tunnels. I am super happy to not have to worry about traffic and crosswalks. In the winter you can stay toasty and in summer you don't have to walk into class all sweaty and hot. The food selection throughout the campus is just right, but I love the fact that if wanted, I could order doordash for certain cravings, and have it delivered to where I am on campus. Maneuvering through city is easy whether it is on foot, scooter or in my car. I commute to school from Avon so going to school or getting back home after is super easy. All of the Administrators, student workers, staff, as well as the teachers I have encountered have all been very helpful and know ledged in what they do. The application and onboarding process was stress free.

I would let other students know that if they want the most out of their college experience, that they should go somewhere else. IU Indianapolis is basically just school, its not like a fun campus to be on, but it does help you prepare for the real world.
I’m not currently in college but I been there a lot, tour or anything I needed they helped me and be there for me

My experience at Indiana University Indianapolis has been pretty good overall. I really like the diverse environment and how supportive most professors are toward students. The campus feels welcoming, and there are plenty of resources to help with academics and mental health. I don’t really have any suggestions for changes, it’s been a positive experience so far.

ai_summary: Indiana University of Pennsylvania offers a vibrant campus experience , highlighted by a beautiful environment and a strong sense of community. Many students appreciate the dedication of professors, who are described as caring and supportive, particularly in helping students navigate their academic paths and fostering personal connections. However, the dining options on campus are a common point of critique, with several reviewers citing a need for greater variety and improved hours . While social life at IUP includes a notable party scene , there are also numerous opportunities for extracurricular involvement, clubs, and networking, which many students find enriching. The campus size is seen as manageable, allowing for easy navigation and personal interactions. Overall, IUP's blend of academic support, campus activities, and a welcoming atmosphere makes it a solid choice for a range of students, though some challenges remain regarding food quality and certain academic experiences.

reviews.text:
Smaller classes
A traditional campus environment
Strong programs in specific fields like business, education, or music.
I need in I up since fall semester, I’m on my 2nd semester. So far my experience has been good in campus.

Indiana University of Pennsylvania is an amazing school! I felt welcomed by my professors aswell as other students. The academics are great, housing is taken care of very well, and the food is also amazing.

It is a pretty good campus. There are tons of student organizations and activities. Small class sizes, which is nice. There are some party people; however, if you don't party, there are activities and people on campus that will work for you. Depending on the department, the fault is nice and will help.
I joined IUP as a dual enrollment student in the summer of 2025 and since then my experience there has been absolutely amazing .

IUP is in a small town in a rural county in PA. I am a student athlete and being in a small town helps keep my focus. There are a lot of student activities to get involved in and you do not have the sprawl of a large campus. The professors are very helpful. I did have a medical emergency last spring and the Administration was very understanding. They have been helping me get back on track after returning this fall.

My experience at Indiana University of Pennsylvania has been great for the most part. I have done well academically, translating to a GPA of 3.19 currently. I have made a ton of life-long friends, as well as, connections to reach out to for the rest of my life. I have also learned many skills to take with me into the future, too. These skills including knowledge surrounding nutrition, time management, discipline, people skills, and many more. I feel that IUP has done well, so far, in setting me up for the next step.

I love the atmosphere of the campus. I enjoy that it is a walking campus which ensures student safety and physical activity. The staff are present and helpful and the professor seek to gave each student strive based on their capabilities. Overall I think IUP is an amazing campus. I do think online programmed courses should be removed when paying for an in person class. That is my only critique.
Indiana university is a very beautiful campus! Everyone here is very helpful! Indiana wants you to succeed at everything. They are very respectful and caring for student life!

I liked going to a new place and being able to learn new things, and It is nice that I can keep doing what I like and being with other people who are so nice and also plan to go in the culinary field.
I love how welcoming the campus students and staff are. The transition from high school to college was fairly easy due to the supports the college has.
It has been a very interesting experience. I have only spent two years here being a transfer student, however, there have been ups and downs during my time here.
The campus is absolutely beautiful and it felt like home. There was a good balance of academics and social life. Staff was friendly and accomidating.

Part of my family has lived in Indiana for years, I've always been around the town and school and I grew to love it. I especially love the size of the campus and how close it is to home, I toured many colleges but IUP was always my first choice. The atmosphere really makes me feel welcomed and comfortable, as a transfer student it could feel awkward coming to a new school but I don't get that feeling with IUP.
I am just entering the college as a freshman...I have visited it several times...loved what I saw...met my professor...loved her...I'm sure this is going to be an amazing experience for me.

ai_summary: Indiana University - Bloomington is known for its beautiful campus and welcoming atmosphere , making it an enjoyable place for students to form friendships and create lasting memories. Academically, the university offers a wide variety of programs, particularly noted for strong departments like the Kelley School of Business, though some students mention that large class sizes can affect engagement. Faculty generally receive positive feedback for their support and dedication, with many professors being praised for their approachability. Student life is vibrant, featuring numerous activities and organizations, although there are mixed feelings about the party culture and Greek life, which some students feel is overwhelming. While the campus is seen as diverse and inclusive , there are concerns about campus safety and mental health support. Reviews also indicate that navigating the large campus can be challenging, especially in inclement weather, and some students are disappointed by the dorm conditions. Overall, IU Bloomington offers a unique blend of academic rigor, social opportunities, and a close-knit community feel despite its size.

reviews.text: I took a tour there on March 10th and I had an amazing experience. The campus is beautiful and the enviornment is everything I wanted. It was accepting and divorce; one of the main reasons I want to go there. The local area of Bloomington is just amazing to walk around, see all the things to do and I'm excited to go out with friends and eat local food and go to local events. The academic portion also is incredible. The amount of classes make me excited to go, because there are so many classes on things I'm interested in: such as a philosophy class on existentialism and marxism; both classes I can take at Indiana University Bloomington.
The ambiance in IU Bloomington is very nice, it's easy to make friends, the courses are very interesting and the university helps me to picture my future
Amazing community and great big campus that still gives a small-town feel. The academics were wonderful.
So far, I've loved my experience at Indiana University. I've been able to meet and make a lot of new people. And I'm very grateful for the opportunity to come here.

My experience at Indiana University Bloomington has been both challenging and rewarding. The university has pushed me to grow academically while giving me access to perspectives, strong faculty support, and meaningful opportunities to explore my interests. Being part of such a collaborative and driven community has helped me develop critical thinking skills and a clearer sense of my goals.
So far my experience here has been very good. My teachers are nice and there's a lot to do in Bloomington, you never get bored.

I couldn't have asked for a better college experience. When I think of IU I think of one word : home. I was so thankful for all of the club opportunities. I was able to try new things, and meet people of all different backgrounds. I genuinely see the world differently than I did before IU. Through the courses I took with amazing professors, I am forever changed. Additionally, I tried new things at school I never thought I would! I went to IU not liking sports and hating the idea of greek life. I ended being someone who got season tickets for basketball and football my sophmore and senior years, and I rushed a sorority! I made lifelong friends, and met the person who I believe I will marry. IU is also a STUNNING campus. I love all of the old architecture : limestone buildings, carved sculptures into the buildings, fountains, wooden floors, and clocks throughout campus. I was a club leader, an RA and I couldn't imagine doing all of those things anywhere else. I blossomed in college!

My experience at Indiana University has truly been one to remember. As a current freshman, I was initially hesitant about adjusting to college life, even though I came from a large high school. The idea of moving away from home felt overwhelming, and I wasn’t sure how long it would take for IU to feel familiar. However, I have never felt so welcomed.
I’ve met so many people through classes, social life, and campus events. Those interactions have helped me build meaningful friendships that provide a strong support system. IU’s community is one of my favorite parts of being here—there is a genuine sense of openness and belonging that makes it easy to connect with others, even in such a large school.

Indiana University in Bloomington Indiana is the most beautiful college campus I have ever seen. Growing up I had the opportunity to visit the campus many times since my dad, brother, grandparents are alumni we found reasons when we were in the area. At first the campus appeared very big and felt overwhelming to think of being able to make it to class. But the reality is that it is perfect. I attend the university now and cannot be happier with my choice for college. I live in a dorm as a freshman and the experience is good. I have been able to meet and make new friends that I would not have in a different setting. The best thing about the dorm is actually the food, it is very good. I cannot complain about any of my professors, there was a big adjustment on my part from high school to college classes and learning how to manage your day with classes spread out. What I love the most about IU is the true college experience for college sports and activities, I love everything about IU.

Indiana University - Bloomington has an amazing International Studies program to support study abroad students, efforts, and international connections which was my personal favorite part of the school. I really enjoyed all of the classes in the Media school and art school as well. Kelley business school is what IUB is known for and while it is certainly a quality education, many of the classes are “weed-out” and you don’t learn things related to your major until your last year of your undergraduate degree. I think that could stand to change and also some of the Kelley teachers are successful in their profession but not cut out for teaching others how to acquire those skills. This all being said the people you meet are lifetime friends, and really hard workers usually. The campus is beautiful, and there is a lot of opportunities to try new things through clubs. All in all the staff outside of Kelley are the best teachers I have encountered as well.

My experience at Indiana University Bloomington has been eye opening to say the least. From the beginning this place has felt like home and the transition from high school to college has been fairly easy for me. The college scenery is beautiful and I love seeing the campus change as the season goes by. Our teams are doing great and statistically this is the best our football team has ever been and I am proud to say I am a part of it. In my opinion this has been a great first year and I genuinely couldn’t name a thing I would change about it!

My experience in the Special Education transition-to-teaching program has been marked by inconsistent communication, limited instructor availability unless I initiate contact, and coursework that does not reflect real-world practice. Instructors often make assumptions about incomplete work without seeking context and give feedback that does not address the assignment requirements. As a student with documented accommodations, support is inconsistent. Any discussion of required adjustments must always be initiated by me, and recommended options for managing workload are not supported. Advising functions the same way—no proactive outreach or regular check-ins. Only one instructor has ever reached out to clarify concerns, and this was described as outside program expectations. Overall, the structure is not accessible for graduate students balancing full-time work and documented academic needs.

I am currently a freshman here at IU, and I have had quite the experience so far. They say college is all about self-discovery, and I can attest to that. I started this year as a pre-business exploratory major, but I changed it within the first week of classes. The environment immediately threw me off. I just wasn't into it, and that's okay. Now, my current goal is to take different classes and meet with career coaches to develop a further understanding of the major I would like to pursue.

Coming to Bloomington was one of the best decisions I ever made. I met so many diverse people from all sorts of backgrounds, ate a variety of different cuisines (Korean food is by far my favorite), and have received so many opportunities to further develop my undergrad education experience.
I have really enjoyed everything at Indiana University, from the dining hall and food options, to the engagement in student life.

Missouri University of Science and Technology
North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
Our Lady of the Lake University

ai_summary: Princeton University is widely recognized for its strong focus on academics and offers a challenging curriculum that pushes students to excel while providing ample resources for support. Many reviews highlight the dedication of professors and the extensive financial aid available, ensuring that diverse student backgrounds are well-supported. Although the academic environment is intense and can be stressful, especially with the culture of grade deflation, students appreciate the collaborative atmosphere and strong sense of community that develops among peers. Student life is vibrant, with numerous clubs and activities, though some note that socializing can sometimes feel cliquish or isolating, particularly for newcomers. The campus is noted for its beauty and safety, and the surrounding town of Princeton adds to the overall experience. While there are concerns about the pressure to succeed and the social scene, many reviewers still regard their time at Princeton as transformative and fulfilling , emphasizing the unique combination of academic rigor and personal growth that the university fosters.

reviews.text:
Princeton would rather pay full tuition for 5800 undergraduates at around $394,000,000 than lose 8 percent of its endowment to taxes and around $3,000,000,000.
At the end of fiscal 2025, the university’s endowment was valued at $35.7 billion, with a payout for the year of $1.8 billion. That represents a spending rate of a little over 5%, according to the university
PRINCO thought they could outsmart the general market in private equities.
The cuts to services and staff benefits are not important to Princeton as it guards its endowment from impact.
Students receiving full aid and yes, even those with family incomes of $250,0000 are pleased. Pell Grant admits are up not due to generosity but from greed of the business that is Princeton.

Princeton University claims to have one of the best computer science departments yet cannot update its own university software for an efficient meal swap program? “The decision (to decommission the meal swap system) was made by the University. However, we were told that the meal exchange software is antiquated and that due to the cost cutting initiative, the University is not in a position to fund the upgrade,” chairman of the Graduate Interclub Council wrote in a statement to The Daily Princetonian.

Max Weiss, a Princeton professor of history and an outspoken advocate of an academic boycott of Is real, led a walkout of his History of Pales tine /Israe l class into McCosh Courtyard, where an anti-Isra el encampment was established. He taught his lesson from there, making him the first faculty member to lecture from the encampment, the Princeton Alumni Weekly reported. Weiss told the publication he hopes the illegal encampment will one day be commemorated with "a plaque."

9 undergraduates and 3 graduate students since 2022 is a concerning number of students. Student athletes are given baseline concussion studies. Princeton students need pre-admission mental health evaluations to match. The atmosphere is not for everyone.

Princeton Grad Student Treasurer Challenged to Subtract From 10,000.
On Monday, the Graduate Student Government (GSG) released the Budget and Finance Report for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year, which revealed that it has already maxed out or exceeded its annual budget in three categories.
Specifically, Events Board contributions, a body in the GSG offering funding for Graduate Student Events, has already exhausted its $10,000 allotment. Special Events — which covers alcohol purchases, materials for events, and catering services — and the Ivy+ Leadership Summit overshot their budgets by $1,476.84 and $823.60, respectively.
On top of these expenses, the report also noted that of the $23,625.00 the GSG had spent on “Beer Jackets,” only $10,519.75 had been recovered in sales.

From the 2025 Report from the Treasurer: “The University is potentially facing a new 8 percent endowment tax, which applies to universities with endowments of over $2 million per student and a tuition-paying population of at least 3,000. With a projected increase of financial aid spending to $327 million in 2025–26, there is a possibility that the University will avoid the endowment tax by having under 3,000 tuition-paying students. The University has previously declined to comment on the endowment tax and the number of students that pay tuition.”
Student life could be improved, but the academics are very comprehensive. All of the courses I have taken so far are interesting.

Thank you to the professors who filed for my Statements for an A+. I fully appreciate this extra effort by professors and I am grateful that my work has been recognized as deserving of the honor at Princeton University!

Princeton University’s President defends higher education on free speech yet the university fails in hiring and admissions to increase free diverse speech.
Eisgruber said universities often reflect what is happening in the culture. "It's on campuses because it's in society," Eisgruber replied. "we are having trouble talking to one another across political differences. Campuses are part of that society, and they're a place where civil discussions and discussions across difference are especially important." When Professor Robert George retires there will be groupthink because of the limited diversity of faculty or students regarding viewpoint. If you are not far left you can expect to keep your views to yourself or face ostracism by faculty and peers. The majority of Princeton graduates self-report that they plan to stay in academia continuing the cycle.

At Princeton you can elect to take a junk course like “Bad Girls.” If you are not paying tuition perhaps you do not mind wasting your time on similar junk courses. If you are a paying tuition I hope you are taking quality courses that will benefit your growth and future employment outside academia.

Admissions standards are not as expected and are varied. One student aced the most rigorous courses in high school, AP 5s, SAT 1590. Another student in the same class had an SAT of 1300 and does not even know something basic as calculating the consumer surplus as an area. The best and the brightest do not opt for test optional nor wish to stand on a soapbox as an activist instead of being a serious academic scholar. Brilliant peers should be admitted for an entire class with a basic expectation of a high level of academic achievement. Grade inflation is a disservice to all students and acceptances not based on merit devalues a diploma’s worth and the reputation of the university.

A new University policy will ban recording on campus with few exceptions starting Jan. 1, raising serious concerns for journalists, among others. It was passed by a wide margin at the Nov. 10 meeting of the Council of the Princeton University Community (CPUC), with no opposing votes.
The policy, which is slightly longer than a page, “prohibits the installation or use of any device for listening, observing, photographing, recording, amplifying, transmitting, or broadcasting sounds or events occurring in any place where the individual or group involved has a reasonable expectation of being free from unwanted surveillance, eavesdropping, recording, or observation without the knowledge and consent of all participants subject to such recordings.” Public or open meetings also may be covered by the no-recording policy “when it has been explicitly stated that recording is not permitted.”

New Princeton students were met with a table of condoms during orientation for an “I Love Female Orgas m” presentation. The students, particularly young women seeking to further their education do not rank being receptacles as top priority of their education.

Several recent incidents of antisemetic graffiti on campus and around town. Where is campus security as the defacing of property occurs? There are cameras everywhere so why haven’t they been identified and caught? Hate is tolerated by inaction. Yet protestors are protected by campus security and town police as they shut down roads and march through Princeton.

“Here is the statement on disruption from the administration — touted as an improvement.
“Going forward, attendees at University events should expect a single warning not to disrupt an event or prevent an invited guest from speaking. Disruptive behavior may result in immediate removal from the event and disciplinary action or other consequences.”
Let me see if I can crack the code. “A single warning” means “one free shot” to disrupt a speaker. “Should expect” translates to “or maybe more, it depends.” And “disruptive behavior may result in ...” signals passivity.
You are either serious about protecting speech or you are not serious. A serious administration might have communicated something like this: “Those who disrupt a speaker will be immediately removed and may face additional consequences.”

ai_summary: The University of Pennsylvania offers a mix of rigorous academics and vibrant student life , making it an attractive option for many high school seniors. Reviewers appreciate the beautiful campus and the diverse range of academic programs, particularly highlighting opportunities in research and cross-disciplinary studies. Many students find the competitive environment both motivating and stressful, often describing a "work hard/play hard" culture that heavily emphasizes pre-professionalism. While the professors are generally praised for their expertise and accessibility, some departments, like math, have faced criticism. Social life at Penn is lively, with numerous events and a strong Greek presence, though the dining options and housing conditions receive mixed reviews. Students express a desire for better mental health support and inclusivity initiatives, indicating areas for potential improvement. Overall, Penn provides a robust educational experience enriched by a culturally dynamic setting in Philadelphia.

reviews.text: Top tier professors. The quality of my education here has far exceeded other colleges and universities I have attended. They are a little but under-resourced for fully online students, but they are growing in that capacity every semester.

I'm not sure if I would attend again because of it's very pre-professional environment. I didn't feel like I was there to learn but rather get a job. I will say, the name recognition of Penn and Wharton has really helped me later on as well.

I love the scene and the sense of community at Penn. The people I've met here are genuinely some of the most interesting and kind individuals. My department is also a highlight—it feels very tight-knit and offers amazing resources for students.

The University of Pennsylvania embodies the perfect balance of “work hard, play hard,” supported by an exceptional network that lasts a lifetime. Its rich traditions and Ivy League legacy create a campus culture where ambition and community thrive together. Every school at Penn offers world‑class academics, with Wharton widely regarded as the best business school in the world. Penn’s dual‑degree options—especially the M&T Program, often considered the most elite undergraduate program in the country—set students up for unparalleled long‑term success. Combined with vibrant student life, spirited athletics, and a genuinely fun college experience, Penn stands out as a truly extraordinary place to grow.
Great academics, social life, city life, and a work hard play hard balance. If you love exploring the city and always having something to do you will love Penn

The University university of Pennsylvania is overall a rigorous institution that maintains Ivy League standards in terms of quality of education, environment, and student experience. The university has lots of resources for almost anything that a student can need, whether it be professional outreach, financial assistance, food assistance, and many other benefits. I find it catering all classes of people. However, some may find its diversity a bit lacking. If it’s your first time trying to get there, you might get confused since it’s in the middle of nowhere. But once you step onto campus, you’ll fall in love with it. If you can handle the extreme workload of multiple papers and assignments every few days as well as the tough grading, then UPenn might be the one for you.
I haven't started yet but from my research university of Pennsylvania is an esteemed university that trains leaders those aspiring to be great with all love, patience and equality

UPenn has provided me with more resources and opportunities than I could ever imagine. It seems like every day I learn about a new service offered at Penn. If I ever had an idea I wanted to pursue (which I do, in fact, have many) I'm assured that there are ample opportunities at Penn to help me bring it to fruition. On top of that, the social scene is amazing here. All the faculty are dedicated towards making sure each student is having a great time and is making friends. Many events are hosted each day where we can cool down and socialize after a long day of classes. It's great, I'm having TONS of fun here while being super intellectually stimulated!

My favorite part of attending Penn is its connections to the Philadelphia community. There are many tutoring services that Penn offers to West Philadelphia elementary and middle schools that allow students to gain a sense of what Philadelphia is like outside of campus. Clubs as well, such as Penn Wellness, serve the community of Philadelphia with food and fun events to help provide access to the public's needs. I would like to see change in healthy food options on campus. Penn offers great options for dining halls, but nutritious food is not always available, nor is variety is food that is offered.
Great university and great campus. People here are fascinating and energetic, love the professors here. But Philly needs to be improved
Good school overall. Campus food needs a lot of work. The professors in the STEM department are not the best, and there is definitely grade deflation. But everything else is amazing.
Penn is the right fit for me- I wanted a "work hard, play hard" place with top notch academics but also a vibrant social life!

My experience at the University of Pennsylvania has been both academically challenging and personally enriching. As a student in the architecture program, I’ve had the opportunity to work closely with passionate faculty, collaborate on complex design projects, and engage in coursework that pushes me to think critically about sustainability, space, and social impact. The diverse campus community has exposed me to new perspectives and helped me grow both as a designer and a global citizen.

As an international student, I’ve had a positive experience at Penn. The school proactively supports students through Campus Express and helpful orientation sessions. I appreciated learning early on about Penn’s safe, accessible transportation and the Career Center’s services—resume reviews, mock interviews, and career advising. Advisors and staff respond promptly, and when visa delays impacted many international students this year, the school provided detailed emails, info sessions, and online course options to keep us on track. However, I hope Penn can offer more scholarships for international students, as tuition is high and financial aid limited. Additionally, while many offices respond efficiently, some departments are understaffed, leading to long delays. Finally, on-campus job opportunities are limited, making it difficult for international students with work restrictions to support themselves. More positions and support for student employment would make a meaningful difference.
Work hard, play hard environment. Help is always available but must be sought out. Philly is a great city!

ai_summary: Yale University offers a compelling mix of rigorous academics and a vibrant student life , appealing to a diverse student body. Many reviews emphasize the supportive community fostered by the residential college system, which encourages close bonds among students and promotes engagement across various extracurricular activities. The professors are frequently noted for their dedication to student success, providing both academic support and mentorship, although some reviews highlight inconsistencies in teaching quality within certain departments, particularly in STEM. Students appreciate the extensive resources available, including peer tutoring and a wide array of clubs and activities, but note the workload can be demanding. The campus is described as both beautiful and inviting, with options for energetic or tranquil environments. While most students cherish their experience, some initial culture shock and social dynamics are noted, especially for those from underrepresented backgrounds. Overall, Yale is recognized for its rich academic culture and strong sense of community, making it a unique and rewarding place to study.

reviews.text: Academics are tough, but there is plenty of support - you just need to ask. My favorite part of Yale is that everyone here takes their academics seriously and I have access to so many opportunities and professors at the top of their field. , but there are also plenty of people like me who also are very social.

This is my first semester at Yale and I am incredibly impressed by all that this school offers. Excellent academics, student life, and endless resources. The infrastructure they have in place for students in terms professional and academic development is quite impressive. As a student, you really feel like you have everything at your fingertips to succeed and thrive, and endless opportunities to explore ideas. The campus is incredible, such beautiful architecture and facilities, and not to mention they cook great food.
The community at Yale is unlike anything I've ever seen before. People here are so kind. I truly feel at home here.
I love it. The classes, professors, academic, extracurricular and professional opportunities are unmatched. The school has the best people and is so much fun and fulfilling.

A few months ago, a friend invited me to sit in on Professor Marci Shore's class, "European Intellectual History Since Nietzsche." After her lecture on Heidegger, I shared an amusing conversation with her students:
"I feel like Instagram is the modern-day Das-man."
"Totally—do you think AI can experience Dasein?"
And as we laughed over lunch about whether Olivia Rodrigo was "authentic" or "inauthentic" by Heidegger's definition, I realized Yalies don't compromise breadth for depth. Obtaining a liberal arts education within a research university framework, I have the unique opportunity to become both a scientist and a citizen. As a student, I'm surrounded by passionate, intellectually and ideologically diverse students at every moment, which makes thought-provoking discussions inevitable.

Attending Yale has been a great opportunity for me. I am from a small town so I've been able to meet people from all different walks of life going to school at Yale. The academic rigor at the school is also definitely present and I've had to change as a student to best fit the academic challenge in my classes.

I've learned that Yale feels a lot smaller than other colleges, for better or worse. It makes for a more intimate college experience, but if you want to really explore and get out into the world, it might not be for you. There are obviously so many resources available to you, but it still feels supported by alumni than other colleges like Harvard and MIT.

Yale has been the best experience of my life. I love the residential college system. The professors are amazing. There are so many activities to do on campus. Nothing but great things to say about my life at Yale and can’t wait for the rest of my time there.
I liked all the resources that were available for first year students. The residential colleges make you feel like you have a family on campus

Yale was a great experience for me as an undergraduate student. I had an excellent academic experience, studying physics and history. I made lifelong friends and tried to make the best of all of the amazing opportunities I was offered through the university!

Great community spirit.
Breadth of courses
All inclusive and welcoming community
Great gym facilities

What I love most about Yale is the access to world-class resources and the inspiring community of peers and professors. The faculty are genuinely invested in students’ success, and the residential college system creates a close-knit, supportive environment.

The overall experience is good. The vibe is very friendly. Everyone is busy but nobody shows off how much they are doing. People genuinely see each other as individual humans and treat each other with kindness.

Positive and a good start to college life. I found the campus to be safe and inviting. I liked how I was able to walk in one direction and find quiet and quaint spaces. If I walked in the other direction I was able to be in the heart of the city. It allowed someone like me to get a mix of high energy or quiet campus life.

being part of the Yale Young Global Scholar from this summer I really appreciate the diversity Yale brings to this campus and the university as a whole. The professors and students that helped with this program have been very welcoming and have showed me a different way of thinking. Showing us the life of a student and lecturing us about interesting topics you would not get taught in high school, Yale has been a great influence in my life.
